statement: Iceland maintains some of the highest agricultural tariffs among OECD countries. The OECD's Producer Support Estimate (PSE) for Iceland was approximately 55–60% of gross farm receipts in 2022–2023, making it one of the highest in the OECD (compared to the EU average of approximately 18–20%). Applied tariff rates on agricultural products average 40–60%, with individual tariffs exceeding 100% on dairy products, meat, and some vegetables. Iceland's agricultural sector received an estimated ISK 20–25 billion annually (approximately EUR 130–160 million) through a combination of direct payments to farmers and border protection. Agriculture accounts for approximately 1% of Iceland's GDP but employs about 2.5–3% of the workforce, with approximately 3,500 farms primarily focused on sheep, dairy, and cattle. EU membership would require dismantling these tariff barriers (as part of the customs union) and replacing Iceland's national support framework with CAP — the fundamental restructuring that has been the primary concern of Bændasamtök Íslands (Icelandic Farmers' Association).
source_name: OECD — Agricultural Policy Monitoring and Evaluation 2023; Hagstofa Íslands; Bændasamtök Íslands
caveats: The PSE is a composite measure that includes both direct budget transfers and market price support (the effect of tariffs and quotas). The high Icelandic PSE partly reflects the large gap between Icelandic and world market prices, which is itself partly a function of high production costs in sub-Arctic conditions. EU membership would replace tariff protection with CAP direct payments, which could partially offset the impact — but the net effect on farm incomes is debated.

related_entries.statement: Food and non-alcoholic beverage prices in Iceland are approximately 50–70% higher than the EU-27 average, according to Eurostat comparative price level indices (2024). This makes Iceland one of the most expensive countries in Europe for food.

Norway, the closest comparator to Iceland as an EEA EFTA state with a protected agricultural sector, spends approximately NOK 20 billion (roughly €1.7 billion) per year on agricultural subsidies and maintains tariffs of 100–400% on key products (dairy, meat). Norway's OECD Producer Support Estimate is approximately 55%, similar to Iceland's. Despite not being in the EU, Norway has gradually liberalised some agricultural trade through bilateral agreements with the EU (Article 19 reviews under the EEA Agreement).

Organic farming in Iceland is minimal, covering less than 1% of agricultural land as of 2024, compared to an EU average of approximately 10% (2022) with an EU target of 25% by 2030 under the Farm to Fork Strategy. Iceland has no specific national organic farming strategy. The EU's Green Deal and Farm to Fork Strategy set binding targets for pesticide reduction (50% by 2030), organic land share, and nutrient loss reduction that would apply to Iceland upon EU membership.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Meðal varnarlína Íslands í landbúnaðarmálum voru kröfur um að tollvernd yrði haldið áfram.
Ráðherra landbúnaðarmála gerði tollverndina að forgangsmáli sínu í aðildarviðræðunum.
Ísland þarf að byggja upp bæði nýja landbúnaðarstefnu og nýtt stofnana- og eftirlitskerfi með landbúnaðinum til að uppfylla kröfur ESB.
Ísland vildi fá sérreglur eða aðlögunartíma og viðhalda tollvernd og stuðningskerfum í landbúnaði.
